---

title: "Why Activation Functions Are Needed"
description: "Understanding how a small non-linear operation lets stacked weighted sums learn more flexible patterns."
format:
    html:
        toc: true
        toc-depth: 2
        code-fold: true
        code-summary: "Show code"
jupyter: python3
---

The previous chapter ended with a missing piece.

Stacking weighted sums does not create a more flexible model by itself.

Something has to happen between those weighted sums.

That missing operation is called an **activation function**.

---

## What an activation function does

A weighted sum creates a score.

```text
score = w1x1 + w2x2 + b
```

An activation function takes that score and changes it before the next calculation step.

```text
output = activation(score)
```

The important part is not the name.

The important part is the effect.

A weighted sum gives a straight-line calculation.

An activation function adds a non-linear change.

That non-linear change is what allows a model to build patterns that are not just straight lines.

In simple terms:

```text
weighted sum → score
activation   → transformed score
```

Or:

```text
weighted sum gives direction
activation adds shape
```

---

## Same score, different activation

The same score can produce very different outputs depending on the activation function.

For example, suppose the weighted score is:

```text
score = 2
```

Different activations transform it differently:

```text
Step(2)    = 1
Sigmoid(2) ≈ 0.88
Tanh(2)    ≈ 0.96
ReLU(2)    = 2
```

Now try changing the score below.

Watch how each activation responds.

```{=html}
<div id="activation-shape-sandbox" class="activation-sandbox">
  <div class="activation-row activation-top-row">
    <div class="activation-segmented-control" aria-label="Activation selector">
      <button id="activation-step-button" type="button">Step</button>
      <button id="activation-sigmoid-button" type="button" class="active">Sigmoid</button>
      <button id="activation-tanh-button" type="button">Tanh</button>
      <button id="activation-relu-button" type="button">ReLU</button>
    </div>
    <div id="activation-output-badge" class="activation-metric-badge">Output: --</div>
  </div>

  <canvas id="activation-canvas" width="720" height="420" class="activation-canvas"></canvas>

  <div class="activation-slider-grid">
    <label>score: <span id="activation-score-value"></span>
      <input id="activation-score-slider" type="range" min="-6" max="6" step="0.1" value="0">
    </label>
  </div>

  <div id="activation-equation" class="activation-equation-box"></div>
  <p id="activation-note" class="activation-note"></p>
</div>

<style>
.activation-sandbox {
  max-width: 820px;
  margin: 1.5rem 0;
  padding: 1rem;
  border: 1px solid #ddd;
  border-radius: 14px;
  background: #fff;
}

.activation-row {
  display: flex;
  gap: 1rem;
  align-items: center;
  flex-wrap: wrap;
}

.activation-top-row {
  justify-content: space-between;
  margin-bottom: 1rem;
}

.activation-segmented-control {
  display: inline-flex;
  border: 1px solid #aaa;
  border-radius: 999px;
  overflow: hidden;
  flex-wrap: wrap;
}

.activation-segmented-control button {
  padding: 0.5rem 0.9rem;
  border: 0;
  background: #fff;
  color: #222;
  cursor: pointer;
  font-size: 0.95rem;
}

.activation-segmented-control button.active {
  background: #222;
  color: #fff;
}

.activation-metric-badge {
  font-weight: 700;
  padding: 0.4rem 0.75rem;
  border-radius: 999px;
  background: #f3f3f3;
}

.activation-canvas {
  width: 100%;
  max-width: 720px;
  border: 1px solid #ddd;
  border-radius: 12px;
  display: block;
  background: #fff;
}

.activation-slider-grid {
  margin-top: 1rem;
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(220px, 1fr));
  gap: 0.9rem;
}

.activation-slider-grid label {
  font-size: 0.95rem;
}

.activation-slider-grid input {
  width: 100%;
}

.activation-equation-box {
  margin-top: 1rem;
  padding: 0.75rem;
  border-radius: 10px;
  background: #f7f7f7;
  font-family: ui-monospace, SFMono-Regular, Menlo, Consolas, monospace;
  overflow-x: auto;
}

.activation-note {
  margin: 0.75rem 0 0;
  font-size: 0.95rem;
}
</style>

<script>
(function () {
  const canvas = document.getElementById("activation-canvas");
  const ctx = canvas.getContext("2d");

  const scoreSlider = document.getElementById("activation-score-slider");
  const scoreValue = document.getElementById("activation-score-value");
  const outputBadge = document.getElementById("activation-output-badge");
  const equationBox = document.getElementById("activation-equation");
  const note = document.getElementById("activation-note");

  const buttons = {
    step: document.getElementById("activation-step-button"),
    sigmoid: document.getElementById("activation-sigmoid-button"),
    tanh: document.getElementById("activation-tanh-button"),
    relu: document.getElementById("activation-relu-button")
  };

  const width = canvas.width;
  const height = canvas.height;

  const xMin = -6;
  const xMax = 6;
  const yMin = -1.5;
  const yMax = 6;

  let selectedActivation = "sigmoid";

  function step(x) {
    return x >= 0 ? 1 : 0;
  }

  function sigmoid(x) {
    return 1 / (1 + Math.exp(-x));
  }

  function tanh(x) {
    return Math.tanh(x);
  }

  function relu(x) {
    return Math.max(0, x);
  }

  function activate(x) {
    if (selectedActivation === "step") return step(x);
    if (selectedActivation === "sigmoid") return sigmoid(x);
    if (selectedActivation === "tanh") return tanh(x);
    return relu(x);
  }

  function toCanvasX(x) {
    return ((x - xMin) / (xMax - xMin)) * width;
  }

  function toCanvasY(y) {
    return height - ((y - yMin) / (yMax - yMin)) * height;
  }

  function drawAxes() {
    ctx.strokeStyle = "#aaa";
    ctx.lineWidth = 1;

    const zeroX = toCanvasX(0);
    const zeroY = toCanvasY(0);

    ctx.beginPath();
    ctx.moveTo(0, zeroY);
    ctx.lineTo(width, zeroY);
    ctx.stroke();

    ctx.beginPath();
    ctx.moveTo(zeroX, 0);
    ctx.lineTo(zeroX, height);
    ctx.stroke();

    ctx.fillStyle = "#666";
    ctx.font = "12px sans-serif";

    for (let x = -6; x <= 6; x += 2) {
      const px = toCanvasX(x);
      ctx.fillRect(px, zeroY - 4, 1, 8);
      ctx.fillText(String(x), px - 6, zeroY + 18);
    }

    for (let y = -1; y <= 6; y += 1) {
      const py = toCanvasY(y);
      ctx.fillRect(zeroX - 4, py, 8, 1);
      if (y !== 0) {
        ctx.fillText(String(y), zeroX + 8, py + 4);
      }
    }

    ctx.fillText("score", width - 45, zeroY - 8);
    ctx.fillText("output", zeroX + 8, 14);
  }

  function drawCurve() {
    ctx.strokeStyle = "#222";
    ctx.lineWidth = 3;
    ctx.beginPath();

    for (let i = 0; i <= 600; i++) {
      const x = xMin + (i / 600) * (xMax - xMin);
      const y = activate(x);
      const px = toCanvasX(x);
      const py = toCanvasY(y);

      if (i === 0) {
        ctx.moveTo(px, py);
      } else {
        ctx.lineTo(px, py);
      }
    }

    ctx.stroke();
  }

  function drawSelectedPoint(score, output) {
    const px = toCanvasX(score);
    const py = toCanvasY(output);

    ctx.setLineDash([6, 5]);
    ctx.strokeStyle = "#777";
    ctx.lineWidth = 1.5;

    ctx.beginPath();
    ctx.moveTo(px, toCanvasY(0));
    ctx.lineTo(px, py);
    ctx.stroke();

    ctx.beginPath();
    ctx.moveTo(toCanvasX(0), py);
    ctx.lineTo(px, py);
    ctx.stroke();

    ctx.setLineDash([]);

    ctx.beginPath();
    ctx.arc(px, py, 7, 0, Math.PI * 2);
    ctx.fillStyle = "#4f6fff";
    ctx.fill();
    ctx.strokeStyle = "#222";
    ctx.lineWidth = 2;
    ctx.stroke();
  }

  function updateButtons() {
    Object.keys(buttons).forEach(function (name) {
      buttons[name].classList.toggle("active", name === selectedActivation);
    });
  }

  function updateText(score, output) {
    const roundedScore = score.toFixed(1);
    const roundedOutput = output.toFixed(3);

    scoreValue.textContent = roundedScore;
    outputBadge.textContent = "Output: " + roundedOutput;

    if (selectedActivation === "step") {
      equationBox.textContent = "output = Step(score) = score >= 0 ? 1 : 0";
      note.textContent = "Step gives a hard jump. It is easy to understand, but too abrupt for smooth learning.";
    } else if (selectedActivation === "sigmoid") {
      equationBox.textContent = "output = Sigmoid(score) = 1 / (1 + e^(-score))";
      note.textContent = "Sigmoid squeezes any score into a value between 0 and 1. It is useful when the final answer should look like a probability.";
    } else if (selectedActivation === "tanh") {
      equationBox.textContent = "output = Tanh(score)";
      note.textContent = "Tanh squeezes values between -1 and 1. Unlike sigmoid, it keeps negative and positive directions.";
    } else {
      equationBox.textContent = "output = ReLU(score) = max(0, score)";
      note.textContent = "ReLU blocks negative scores and keeps positive scores unchanged. This simple behavior is widely used inside deep models.";
    }
  }

  function draw() {
    const score = parseFloat(scoreSlider.value);
    const output = activate(score);

    ctx.clearRect(0, 0, width, height);
    drawAxes();
    drawCurve();
    drawSelectedPoint(score, output);
    updateButtons();
    updateText(score, output);
  }

  Object.keys(buttons).forEach(function (name) {
    buttons[name].addEventListener("click", function () {
      selectedActivation = name;
      draw();
    });
  });

  scoreSlider.addEventListener("input", draw);

  draw();
})();
</script>
```

This is the first key idea.

The weighted score is the same kind of number.

But different activations pass that number forward in different ways.

So the activation function changes how information flows through the model.

---

## Why not just use non-linear equations?

At this point, a natural question appears:

> If we need curves, why not directly write curved equations?

For example:

```text
score = x1² + x2²
```

Or:

```text
score = sin(x1) + x2²
```

This can work if we already know the shape of the problem.

If the data follows a circle, squared terms may help.

If the data follows a wave, a sine function may help.

But real problems usually do not tell us the correct equation in advance.

We may not know whether the pattern is circular, layered, repeated, broken into regions, or something more complex.

So instead of manually choosing one big non-linear equation, neural networks use a more flexible method:

```text
weighted sum → activation → weighted sum → activation → ...
```

Each weighted sum learns a simple score.

Each activation bends the score.

Together, they can build more complex shapes from small reusable parts.

That is more flexible than guessing one hand-written equation upfront.

---

## Activation between weighted sums

In the previous chapter, stacking weighted sums still resulted in one straight-line decision.

The missing piece is to place a non-linear operation between those weighted sums.

Without activation:

```text
score1 = w11x1 + w12x2 + b1
score2 = w21x1 + w22x2 + b2

final_score = a score1 + d score2 + c
```

Because everything is still multiplication and addition, the final result can collapse back into one straight-line equation.

With activation:

```text
score1 = w11x1 + w12x2 + b1
score2 = w21x1 + w22x2 + b2

h1 = activation(score1)
h2 = activation(score2)

final_score = a h1 + d h2 + c
```

Now the final step is no longer combining raw straight-line scores.

It is combining transformed scores.

That is what stops the model from behaving like just one straight line.

---

## How activation helps with XOR

The XOR pattern cannot be solved by one straight line.

The previous chapter showed that.

Now we can use two internal weighted sums, apply activation to each one, and combine the transformed outputs.

For this example, we will use ReLU.

```text
h1 = ReLU(x1 + x2)
h2 = ReLU(-x1 - x2)

final_score = h1 + h2 - 2
```

This is not the only way to build a non-linear boundary.

The goal here is not to find the best model.

The goal is to see what activation changes.

Try the sandbox below.

Switch between **No Activation** and **With ReLU**.

```{=html}
<div id="xor-activation-sandbox" class="activation-sandbox">
  <div class="activation-row activation-top-row">
    <div class="activation-segmented-control" aria-label="XOR model selector">
      <button id="xor-no-activation-button" type="button">No Activation</button>
      <button id="xor-relu-button" type="button" class="active">With ReLU</button>
    </div>
    <div id="xor-accuracy" class="activation-metric-badge">Accuracy: --</div>
  </div>

  <canvas id="xor-activation-canvas" width="640" height="440" class="activation-canvas"></canvas>

  <div class="activation-slider-grid">
    <label>bias: <span id="xor-bias-value"></span>
      <input id="xor-bias-slider" type="range" min="-6" max="6" step="0.1" value="-2">
    </label>
  </div>

  <div id="xor-equation" class="activation-equation-box"></div>
  <p id="xor-note" class="activation-note"></p>
</div>

<script>
(function () {
  const canvas = document.getElementById("xor-activation-canvas");
  const ctx = canvas.getContext("2d");

  const noActivationButton = document.getElementById("xor-no-activation-button");
  const reluButton = document.getElementById("xor-relu-button");
  const biasSlider = document.getElementById("xor-bias-slider");
  const biasValue = document.getElementById("xor-bias-value");
  const accuracy = document.getElementById("xor-accuracy");
  const equation = document.getElementById("xor-equation");
  const note = document.getElementById("xor-note");

  const width = canvas.width;
  const height = canvas.height;
  const scale = 46;
  const originX = width / 2;
  const originY = height / 2;

  let useRelu = true;

  const points = [
    { x: -2, y: -2, label: 0 },
    { x: -2, y:  2, label: 1 },
    { x:  2, y: -2, label: 1 },
    { x:  2, y:  2, label: 0 }
  ];

  function relu(x) {
    return Math.max(0, x);
  }

  function toCanvasX(x) {
    return originX + x * scale;
  }

  function toCanvasY(y) {
    return originY - y * scale;
  }

  function internalScores(x, y) {
    return {
      score1: x + y,
      score2: -x - y
    };
  }

  function finalScore(x, y, bias) {
    const scores = internalScores(x, y);

    if (useRelu) {
      const h1 = relu(scores.score1);
      const h2 = relu(scores.score2);
      return h1 + h2 + bias;
    }

    return scores.score1 + scores.score2 + bias;
  }

  function predict(x, y, bias) {
    return finalScore(x, y, bias) >= 0 ? 1 : 0;
  }

  function drawAxes() {
    ctx.strokeStyle = "#aaa";
    ctx.lineWidth = 1;

    ctx.beginPath();
    ctx.moveTo(0, originY);
    ctx.lineTo(width, originY);
    ctx.stroke();

    ctx.beginPath();
    ctx.moveTo(originX, 0);
    ctx.lineTo(originX, height);
    ctx.stroke();

    ctx.fillStyle = "#666";
    ctx.font = "12px sans-serif";

    for (let i = -6; i <= 6; i++) {
      const x = toCanvasX(i);
      const y = toCanvasY(i);

      ctx.fillRect(x, originY - 3, 1, 6);
      ctx.fillRect(originX - 3, y, 6, 1);

      if (i !== 0) {
        ctx.fillText(String(i), x - 4, originY + 16);
        ctx.fillText(String(i), originX + 8, y + 4);
      }
    }
  }

  function drawRegions(bias) {
    const cell = 8;

    for (let px = 0; px < width; px += cell) {
      for (let py = 0; py < height; py += cell) {
        const x = (px - originX) / scale;
        const y = (originY - py) / scale;
        const cls = predict(x, y, bias);

        ctx.fillStyle = cls === 1
          ? "rgba(80, 120, 255, 0.12)"
          : "rgba(255, 140, 80, 0.12)";

        ctx.fillRect(px, py, cell, cell);
      }
    }
  }

  function drawInternalLines() {
    ctx.setLineDash([8, 6]);
    ctx.strokeStyle = "#777";
    ctx.lineWidth = 1.5;

    drawLine(1, 1, 0);
    drawLine(-1, -1, 0);

    ctx.setLineDash([]);
  }

  function drawLine(w1, w2, b) {
    if (Math.abs(w1) < 0.0001 && Math.abs(w2) < 0.0001) {
      return;
    }

    ctx.beginPath();

    if (Math.abs(w2) < 0.0001) {
      const x = -b / w1;
      ctx.moveTo(toCanvasX(x), 0);
      ctx.lineTo(toCanvasX(x), height);
    } else {
      const xLeft = -8;
      const xRight = 8;
      const yLeft = -(w1 * xLeft + b) / w2;
      const yRight = -(w1 * xRight + b) / w2;
      ctx.moveTo(toCanvasX(xLeft), toCanvasY(yLeft));
      ctx.lineTo(toCanvasX(xRight), toCanvasY(yRight));
    }

    ctx.stroke();
  }

  function drawPoints(bias) {
    points.forEach(function (point) {
      const predicted = predict(point.x, point.y, bias);
      const isCorrect = predicted === point.label;

      ctx.beginPath();
      ctx.arc(toCanvasX(point.x), toCanvasY(point.y), 8, 0, Math.PI * 2);
      ctx.fillStyle = point.label === 1 ? "#4f6fff" : "#ff8a4f";
      ctx.fill();
      ctx.lineWidth = isCorrect ? 2 : 4;
      ctx.strokeStyle = isCorrect ? "#222" : "#d00000";
      ctx.stroke();

      ctx.fillStyle = "#111";
      ctx.font = "12px sans-serif";
      ctx.fillText("class " + point.label, toCanvasX(point.x) + 10, toCanvasY(point.y) - 10);
    });
  }

  function updateAccuracy(bias) {
    let correct = 0;

    points.forEach(function (point) {
      if (predict(point.x, point.y, bias) === point.label) {
        correct += 1;
      }
    });

    const pct = Math.round((correct / points.length) * 100);
    accuracy.textContent = "Accuracy: " + pct + "% (" + correct + "/" + points.length + " correct)";
  }

  function updateButtons() {
    noActivationButton.classList.toggle("active", !useRelu);
    reluButton.classList.toggle("active", useRelu);
  }

  function updateText(bias) {
    biasValue.textContent = bias.toFixed(1);

    if (useRelu) {
      equation.textContent = "h1 = ReLU(x1 + x2), h2 = ReLU(-x1 - x2), final_score = h1 + h2 + " + bias.toFixed(1);
      note.textContent = "With ReLU, the internal scores are transformed before they are combined. The decision region is no longer forced to behave like one straight line.";
    } else {
      equation.textContent = "final_score = (x1 + x2) + (-x1 - x2) + " + bias.toFixed(1);
      note.textContent = "Without activation, the internal scores cancel into a simple constant here. More generally, stacked weighted sums still collapse into one linear calculation.";
    }
  }

  function draw() {
    const bias = parseFloat(biasSlider.value);

    ctx.clearRect(0, 0, width, height);
    drawRegions(bias);
    drawAxes();
    drawInternalLines();
    drawPoints(bias);

    updateAccuracy(bias);
    updateButtons();
    updateText(bias);
  }

  noActivationButton.addEventListener("click", function () {
    useRelu = false;
    draw();
  });

  reluButton.addEventListener("click", function () {
    useRelu = true;
    draw();
  });

  biasSlider.addEventListener("input", draw);

  draw();
})();
</script>
```

The dashed lines show the internal weighted sums.

When there is no activation, those internal calculations do not create a useful non-linear shape.

When ReLU is added, each internal score is transformed before the final step combines them.

That transformation is the key.

The activation function does not magically solve every problem.

But it gives the model a new ability:

```text
combine transformed scores instead of only combining raw straight-line scores
```

That is what allows deeper models to become useful.

---

## Different activations behave differently

Not all activations do the same thing.

They all add non-linearity, but they shape the score differently.

Let us compare two important ones:

```text
Sigmoid
ReLU
```

### Sigmoid squeezes

Sigmoid takes any score and squeezes it between 0 and 1.

```text
very negative score → close to 0
score near 0        → close to 0.5
very positive score → close to 1
```

This is useful when the final output should behave like a probability.

For example:

```text
0.91 → likely class 1
0.12 → likely class 0
```

But sigmoid can become weak inside many layers.

When the score is already very positive or very negative, the output barely changes.

```text
Sigmoid(8) ≈ 0.9997
Sigmoid(9) ≈ 0.9999
```

Both values are almost the same.

So large scores get squeezed into a tiny output range.

That can make learning slower inside deep models.

### ReLU filters

ReLU behaves differently.

```text
ReLU(score) = max(0, score)
```

So:

```text
negative score → 0
positive score → same value
```

ReLU does not squeeze positive scores into a small range.

A strong positive score stays strong.

That makes ReLU useful inside hidden layers.

But ReLU also has a weakness.

If the score is negative, the output becomes exactly zero.

If some part of the model keeps producing negative scores, it may stop contributing.

That is why variants like Leaky ReLU exist.

---

## Sigmoid vs ReLU on the same scores

Use the table below to compare the same input scores.

```{python}
import math
import pandas as pd

def sigmoid(x):
    """Return the sigmoid activation value."""
    return 1 / (1 + math.exp(-x))

def relu(x):
    """Return the ReLU activation value."""
    return max(0, x)

scores = [-3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3]

comparison = pd.DataFrame({
    "score": scores,
    "sigmoid(score)": [round(sigmoid(x), 3) for x in scores],
    "relu(score)": [relu(x) for x in scores],
})

comparison
```

The table shows the difference clearly.

Sigmoid compresses all values into a small range between 0 and 1.

ReLU removes negative values and keeps positive values open.

That difference affects where each activation is useful.

---

## A practical view of common activation functions

We do not need to memorize every activation function now.

For this chapter, focus on the behavior.

| Activation | Main behavior                                | Common use                                        |
| ---------- | -------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| Step       | Hard yes/no jump                             | Historical explanation, simple decision intuition |
| Sigmoid    | Squeezes values between 0 and 1              | Binary output                                     |
| Tanh       | Squeezes values between -1 and 1             | Centered signals, older networks                  |
| ReLU       | Keeps positive values, zeros negative values | Hidden layers in many deep models                 |
| Softmax    | Converts multiple scores into probabilities  | Multi-class output                                |

The most important one for now is ReLU.

It is simple.

It adds non-linearity.

It allows positive signals to keep their strength.

That combination made it very useful in deep learning.

---

## Where different activations are used

A useful rule of thumb:

```text
Use sigmoid when the final answer is a binary probability-like output.

Use softmax when the final answer must choose one class from many classes.

Use ReLU-like activations inside hidden layers.
```

For example:

```text
Binary classification:
final output → sigmoid

Multi-class classification:
final output → softmax

Internal layers:
hidden outputs → ReLU or a ReLU-like activation
```

This is not a universal law.

But it is a good beginner mental model.

The activation should match the role of that step.

---

## Main takeaway

A weighted sum creates a score.

An activation function changes that score before the next calculation step.

That change matters because it adds non-linearity.

Without activation:

```text
weighted sum → weighted sum → weighted sum
```

the model still behaves like a linear calculation.

With activation:

```text
weighted sum → activation → weighted sum → activation
```

the model can build more flexible patterns.

Different activation functions change scores differently.

So activation functions are not just mathematical decoration.

They decide how scores become useful signals.

That is why deep learning depends on them.

---

## Chapter summary

```text
An activation function transforms a weighted score.

output = activation(score)

This non-linear transformation prevents stacked weighted sums from behaving like one straight-line calculation.

Sigmoid squeezes values between 0 and 1.

ReLU blocks negative values and keeps positive values unchanged.

Softmax is usually used when choosing one class from many classes.

The key idea:
activation functions let models combine transformed signals, not just raw linear scores.
```

Next chapter:

```text
Why Layers Are Needed
```
